title: Anno Didnt Say This Anno Didnt Know That Not Canon Etc
display_title: Anno Didnt Say This Anno Didnt Know That Not Canon Etc
wikitext:
[]: Anno Didn't Say This; Anno Didn't Know That; Not Canon Etc. Post by J_Faulkner » Thu Apr 22, 2010 9:59 pm Littera gesta docet, quid credes allegoria, Moralis quid agas, quo tendas anagogia. * I've often come across Evangelion threads when interpretations using existing theories and ideas are dismissed simply because there is no evidence Anno knew about them. "Anno didn't say this. Who cares." This kind of reasoning never fails to astound me. When we make an interpretation of Evangelion using existing ideas from X, we don't change the underlying material, but recast them in a new light. Now, sometimes the light is so dim that the interpretative framework is essentially useless, probably because it has little to no relation to the themes, ideas, characters etc. in Evangelion. But the point is that the light is being shone by the person doing the interpreting and not the person whose work is being interpreted. It makes no sense to dismiss the shining light by appealing to the creator(s) possible ignorance of the light being used, provided the creator(s) didn't outright dismiss the framework being used. This is like dismissing the relevance of the Bible to South Korean communities because the original authors didn't know anything about them. You can only dismiss such an interpretation by showing the superficiality of the insights gained under the shining light. Remember that Anno himself gave a lot of leeway for interpretation: Evangelion is like a puzzle, you know. Any person can see it and give his/her own answer. In other words, we're offering viewers to think by themselves, so that each person can imagine his/her own world. We will never offer the answers, even in the theatrical version.

[]: Post by Teague » Thu Apr 22, 2010 10:38 pm I'll second this notion. Any interpretation of a given work is equally valid if there is evidence within the work to back up said interpretation, regardless of the author's, publisher's or anyone else's intent. Anything that could be, can be.

[]: Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Thu Apr 22, 2010 11:25 pm I'm not entirely sure what the main point of this thread is? Are you saying that people should not delve so deeply when analysising Evangelion? Or that they should? Could you give some examples of what you mean when you say a theory is dismissed because it is unlikely Anno knew about it? Teague wrote: I'll second this notion. Any interpretation of a given work is equally valid if there is evidence within the work to back up said interpretation, regardless of the author's, publisher's or anyone else's intent. Anything that could be, can be.

[]: Post by DIDDY » Thu Apr 22, 2010 11:59 pm ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: I'm not entirely sure what the main point of this thread is?

[]: Post by Sachi » Fri Apr 23, 2010 12:06 am This reminds me of that one thread with people shouting "UR NOT ANNO!" at each other. Basically, if you see something in the show and you can conjure up enough evidence to support it, it is a valid enough interpretation, even if the creators didn't think of it while making the media. This applies not only to Evangelion, but to any work of art, be it poetry, literature, or anything.

[]: Post by SenorSquiid » Fri Apr 23, 2010 12:13 am ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: I think that's going too far; postmodernism run amok.

[]: Post by esselfortium » Fri Apr 23, 2010 12:13 am As far as theories go, the sky is the limit. As far as trying to determine things that would actually be considered an important and factual part of the show, their importance can be significantly diminished if there is little or no evidence that they were planned or done intentionally. For instance, (correct me if I'm wrong) I'm pretty sure Lilith in Terminal Dogma was still referred to as Adam as late in production as the episode 24 drafts. This, combined with the fact that much of the seemingly-vital CI was not mentioned or alluded to in any of the 13 hours Anno was allotted to tell his story, suggests to me that Eva's mythology was more or less improvised as they went along, and--while it certainly serves as an interesting supplement--mostly had no real influence on the show. Which is, of course, not an inherently bad thing and doesn't diminish its existence as a part of Eva's backstory, but I do feel sometimes that this information is given a bit too much importance for something that was only revealed as unlockable bonus content in a video game made 10 years after the show.

[]: Post by UrsusArctos » Fri Apr 23, 2010 12:17 am esselfortium wrote: For instance, (correct me if I'm wrong) I'm pretty sure Lilith in Terminal Dogma was still referred to as Adam as late in production as the episode 24 drafts.

[]: Post by SenorSquiid » Fri Apr 23, 2010 12:30 am UrsusArctos wrote: From what I recall, Lilith was referred to by name in the script for Episode 15, where Misato first sees her crucified body. Don't forget that the Second Angel had been missing since the very beginning. However, they did make changes along the way. "Essene" was the name initially used in the scripts for Seele, but they changed it sometime early in the show's run.

[]: Post by Reichu » Fri Apr 23, 2010 12:31 am UrsusArctos wrote: From what I recall, Lilith was referred to by name in the script for Episode 15, where Misato first sees her crucified body.

[]: Post by DIDDY » Fri Apr 23, 2010 2:27 am Oh and btw: ObsessiveMathsFreak wrote: I think that's going too far; postmodernism run amok. If we take that view, all works become meaningless.

[]: Post by SenorSquiid » Fri Apr 23, 2010 2:30 am You see, the Evangelion is actually a Penis...

[]: Post by UrsusArctos » Fri Apr 23, 2010 2:36 am Reichu wrote: If you're talking about the actual episode, Misato goes, "Eva? [cue 2I flashback] Durr, no--!"

[]: Post by Reichu » Fri Apr 23, 2010 5:59 am UrsusArctos wrote: Not that I've forgotten her reaction, or Kaji's affirmation that the giant was Adam. Just that somewhere it was spelt out clearly that Lilith, not Adam, was on the cross. Perhaps it was the script for 24?

[]: Post by skikes » Fri Apr 23, 2010 6:50 am I think we're all aware that there is a certain amount of give and take in regards to this subject. Yes there can be a evidence that the creators didn't intentionally put in there that is eventually regarded as canon but we can't put everything down to "well that's how I interrupted it." Otherwise we end up with things such as "Asuka and Rei were totally gay for eachother" and "Ritsuko was raped by Seele but not really Seele, by Seele henchmen."

[]: Post by TheLobe » Fri Apr 23, 2010 8:28 am In every thread someone always says that either anno didn't intend that or that it's probably a mistake. I agree that saying it wasn't annos view is stupid. The point of having things open for interpretation is so you yourself can interpret.

[]: Post by thewayneiac » Fri Apr 23, 2010 10:08 am Oh, this again. Nothing in the field of literary interpretation ticks me off more than this "all interpretations are equally valid" and "once a work is complete the interpretation no longer belongs to the author" business. If the author fails to get his point across, the proper thing to study is "How did he fail to communicate?" "Is he a poor communicator? In what way did he fail?" Reader or viewer misreadings should be treated as irrelevant. The notion that a reader interpretaion that contradicts the author's stated intent is equally valid is elitist garbage. (I actually had one teacher who told me that reader interpretations were more valid than the author's.) A work that can literally mean anything means nothing. When Anno challanged the viewers to find their own answers he was not saying there were no correct answers, just that he wasn't providing them.

[]: Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Fri Apr 23, 2010 11:27 am DIDDY wrote: Could you explain what you mean by "meaningless"?

[]: Post by Hoshikawa-kun » Fri Apr 23, 2010 12:13 pm While I do think it clear that there is room for more than merely Anno's views, I believe this because it has been indicated that Eva is to be interpreted by each individual. There is the 'original meaning' that he intended to portray, and then there is the 'self meaning' that each individual finds when they watch. The viewer is going through a journey just as Shinji is(though on that note, there could also be an 'Anno's meaning' that is Anno's journey, separate from the 'original meaning'. He indicated that Shinji is essentially him at 14, blown out of proportion, so perhaps Anno found a personal meaning in Eva beyond the scope of his goals?) At the same time however, I would like to point this out. Anno is not the only authority on Eva. Anno was the director, but he was also part of a team, and that team's views are valid as well because they also contributed to the script and had their own ideas. If you'll recall, Anno wasn't even involved in making Episode 04's script, yet it is still considered important to Shinji's development as a character. So I would suggest looking at interpretations from people that were involved with Eva but not Anno. I'm against the 'any interpretation goes' idea despite what I said about self meaning. If you want to say that Evangelion is a story about the glory of Fascism, I better see Shinji with a toothbrush mustache and Gendo cosplaying Mussolini.

[]: Post by ran1 » Fri Apr 23, 2010 12:39 pm Hoshikawa-kun wrote: I'm against the 'any interpretation goes' idea despite what I said about self meaning. If you want to say that Evangelion is a story about the glory of Fascism, I better see Shinji with a toothbrush mustache and Gendo cosplaying Mussolini.
